graph.confusable_reason: Both are dioecious Amaranthus pigweeds with hairless stems and similar seedlings, routinely confused in soybean and corn.
graph.shared_presentation: Tall, smooth-stemmed pigweed with alternate leaves and a terminal seedhead; no hairs on stems or leaves.

forks.question: What is the overall plant habit and branching?
forks.a_signal: Tall, highly branched, robust, almost tree-like, especially in competitive stands.
forks.b_signal: More slender with fewer, more upright branches and a willowy look.

forks.question: Look at mid-canopy leaves: leaf shape and petiole length relative to the blade.
forks.a_signal: Diamond- or spade-shaped leaves wider toward the tip, with the petiole at least as long as or longer than the blade on many leaves.
forks.b_signal: Elongated, narrow, lanceolate leaves with the petiole clearly shorter than the blade.

forks.question: Check fine leaf traits: a single hair at the leaf-tip notch or a faint V-shaped watermark.
forks.a_signal: A small hair at the very leaf-tip notch (when present) and an occasional faint V watermark favor Palmer, but only weakly.
forks.b_signal: No tip hair; these fine marks absent leans waterhemp.

forks.question: If flowering, examine female seedheads and bracts.
forks.a_signal: Very long, thick, tough terminal seedheads; bracts 3-7 mm, stiff, extending beyond the tepals and sharp to the touch.
forks.b_signal: Shorter seedheads; bracts under 3 mm, not extending much beyond the tepals.

graph.narrative_a: Because the plant has diamond-shaped leaves with petioles as long as or longer than the blades, robust branching, and long thick seedheads with stiff bracts, I classify it as Palmer amaranth rather than waterhemp.
graph.narrative_b: Because the leaves are narrow and lanceolate with petioles shorter than the blade, a slender willowy habit, and short bracts, I classify it as waterhemp rather than Palmer amaranth.
graph.management_a: Palmer amaranth: overlap residual herbicides and remove escapes before seed set; assume glyphosate/ALS resistance.
graph.management_b: Waterhemp: same residual-overlap and zero-tolerance approach; both pigweeds are widely herbicide-resistant.
